Tract-Level Analysis: Housing Growth, Demographic Change, and Greenness Exposure

Lilac Hong

2026-04-30

Guiding questions

  1. Where is housing being built and who lives there (and how have demographics changed over time)?
  2. Who is experiencing changes in exposure to green environments?

Demographic categories

  • Race: AIAN, Asian, Black, NHOPI, Two or More, White
  • Age: 0–19, 20–29, 30–59, 60+

Part I: Pseudo-Residual Greenness Analysis

\(\text{NDVI}_{2010} = \alpha + \beta \cdot \log(\text{HU_density}_{2010})\)

  • GHI2010 = NDVI2010 − (\(\hat{\alpha}_{2010}\) + \(\hat{\beta}_{2010}\) · log(HU_density2010))
  • GHI2020 = NDVI2020 − (\(\hat{\alpha}_{2010}\) + \(\hat{\beta}_{2010}\) · log(HU_density2020))
  • ΔGHI = GHI2020 − GHI2010

Sample: 1318 unique tracts.

Table 1 — 2010 baseline regressions

season buffer intercept slope slope_se slope_p R2 n
Dormant_Season 1000 0.5458 -0.0436 0.0014 0 0.4248 1318
Growing_Season 1000 0.6384 -0.0491 0.0013 0 0.5092 1318

At the tract scale, the housing-density–NDVI relationship is strongly negative (slope ≈ −0.044 to −0.049), with R² of 0.42–0.51. This reveals the expected urban signal: denser tracts are measurably less green.

Table 2 — ΔGHI summary

season buffer n mean sd median pct_bright pct_dim
Dormant_Season 1000 1318 -0.0862 0.0992 -0.0694 19.8786 80.1214
Growing_Season 1000 1318 0.0106 0.0189 0.0114 77.7693 22.2307

The two seasons tell fundamentally different stories. In the dormant season, the average tract dimmed by 0.086 NDVI units, and 80% of tracts dimmed — indicating widespread loss of structural and evergreen vegetation visible during leaf-off conditions. In the growing season, the pattern reverses: the average tract brightened slightly (+0.011), and 78% of tracts brightened, suggesting that active-season canopy held or improved even as winter greenness declined. A tract can simultaneously lose dormant-season greenness and gain growing-season greenness; this is not a contradiction but reflects different ecological signals (evergreen/structural cover vs. deciduous canopy cover).

Table 3 — Bright/Dim transitions

season buffer Bright -> Bright Bright -> Dim Dim -> Bright Dim -> Dim
Dormant_Season 1000 232 495 57 534
Growing_Season 1000 759 22 69 468

The dormant-season transition asymmetry is striking: 495 tracts shifted from Bright to Dim, while only 57 moved from Dim to Bright — an 8.7:1 ratio. This is sharper than the block-level asymmetry (7.5:1). In the growing season, Bright→Bright dominates (759 tracts), and the few transitions that occur slightly favor improvement (69 Dim→Bright vs. 22 Bright→Dim).

Greenness figures

Part II: Where Is Housing Being Built and Who Lives There?

Table 4 — Housing growth summary

Value
n_tracts 1318.0
total_HU_2010 2196124.9
total_HU_2020 2451417.0
total_delta_HU 255292.1
pct_gained 91.8
pct_lost 8.2
median_delta_HU 101.7
mean_delta_HU 193.7
total_POP_2010 5102530.9
total_POP_2020 5902734.1
total_delta_POP 800203.2

Washington’s 1,318 analytical tracts gained approximately 255,000 housing units between 2010 and 2020, a net increase of 11.6%. Housing growth was nearly universal: 92% of tracts gained units, while only 8% lost them. However, growth was highly concentrated — the mean gain (194 units) was nearly double the median (102 units), indicating a right-skewed distribution in which a minority of tracts absorbed a disproportionate share of new construction. Population grew by approximately 800,000 over the same period.

hu_growth_q n mean_dHU mean_delta_pct_AIAN mean_delta_pct_Asian mean_delta_pct_Black mean_delta_pct_NHOPI mean_delta_pct_Two_or_More mean_delta_pct_White

delta_pct_60p -0.364

The correlation table confirms the quartile patterns. The strongest demographic predictor of housing growth is the 20–29 age share (r = 0.40 in 2010, r = 0.48 in 2020), followed by the change in 20–29 share (r = 0.38) and the change in Asian share (r = 0.36). Housing growth is negatively correlated with the 60+ share (r = −0.27 in 2020) and with the change in 60+ share (r = −0.36).

Part III: Who Is Experiencing Changes in Exposure to Green Environments?

We stratify greenness outcomes by the demographic composition of the tract to answer: are particular populations disproportionately exposed to greenness loss? We use 2020 demographics (who lives there when the change is realized) as the primary stratifier and use White as an example to test.

Table 7 — ΔGHI by % White tercile (2020)

Growing 1000 m High White 439 0.009 0.019 0.011 80.410 86.329 0.664 0.669 0.005
